articles.title: A Spending-Cap Table Waits for the Justices
articles.summary: The 2026 campaign-finance fight is not only about super PACs or billionaires. A small FEC chart — $65,300 for most House races, $4,071,800 for a California Senate race — could decide how closely party committees and candidates may work together before the midterms.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Spending-Cap Table Waits for the Justices. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Spending-Cap Table Waits for the Justices
articles.visual_archetype: object-detail still life built around a concrete artifact from the reporting
articles.visual_brief: A close, newsroom-style still life on a wooden desk before dawn: a printed Federal Election Commission 2026 coordinated party expenditure limits table with rows for California, Texas, Pennsylvania and the generic House limit, one yellow sticky note reading 'Schedule F / Line 25', a dull pencil, a calculator showing 65300, and a half-folded TV ad storyboard partially tucked under the paper. Shallow depth of field, natural desk-lamp light, no maps, no court building, no political logos.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Spending-Cap Table Waits for the Justices
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Spending-Cap Table Waits for the Justices. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A $321 Million Health Fund Pulls the Writers Room Into Focus
articles.summary: The Writers Guild’s ratified four-year studio contract avoided a near-term writers shutdown. The quieter culture story is more durable: in a smaller Hollywood, health insurance, residual math, AI notice language and enforcement paperwork have become part of how shows get made.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A $321 Million Health Fund Pulls the Writers Room Into Focus. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A $321 Million Health Fund Pulls the Writers Room Into Focus
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: A close, natural-light tabletop scene in a modest writers-room side office: two pairs of hands mark up a printed screenplay with colored pencils while another hand sorts health-plan enrollment pages, residuals statements and contract-summary printouts into labeled folders. A laptop in the background shows a blurred production calendar; coffee rings, sticky notes, a pill organizer, binder clips and a capped highlighter add lived-in detail. No celebrity faces, no studio logos, no picket signs; the image should make the work of writing, revising and administering benefits feel tactile and specific.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A $321 Million Health Fund Pulls the Writers Room Into Focus
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A $321 Million Health Fund Pulls the Writers Room Into Focus. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Toll Tags Caught a GPS Backup in Their Lane
articles.summary: A useful strip of 900 MHz spectrum helps meters report, toll tags answer gantries, warehouses read goods and security devices check in. NextNav wants part of it remade for a terrestrial GPS complement. The fight has become a practical test of how much disturbance Washington will accept in the quiet machinery of everyday wireless life.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Toll Tags Caught a GPS Backup in Their Lane. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Toll Tags Caught a GPS Backup in Their Lane
articles.visual_archetype: layered interior scene seen through glass, doorway, or reflection
articles.visual_brief: Documentary-style interior scene viewed through the slightly reflective glass door of a municipal utility radio test room at night. In the foreground reflection, show a toll transponder sticker on a windshield and faint streetlights outside. Through the glass, show a workbench with an open smart electric meter, a handheld spectrum analyzer, coiled coaxial cables, printed FCC filings with colored tabs, a small RFID reader and a wall monitor showing a low jagged radio-frequency trace around 900 MHz. Use fluorescent room light mixed with instrument glow, shallow depth of field and human-scale detail. Avoid maps, state outlines, server racks, data-center exteriors, glowing AI imagery, courtroom symbols and generic network abstractions.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Toll Tags Caught a GPS Backup in Their Lane
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Toll Tags Caught a GPS Backup in Their Lane. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Grocery Card Has Become a State Budget Line
articles.summary: SNAP is still a monthly food benefit loaded onto an EBT card. But expanded time-limit rules, error-rate penalties and coming state cost shifts are turning grocery aid into a 2026 test of caseworkers, food banks, retailers and governors’ ledgers.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Grocery Card Has Become a State Budget Line. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Grocery Card Has Become a State Budget Line
articles.visual_archetype: wide environmental scene showing infrastructure, landscape, or atmosphere
articles.visual_brief: A wide early-morning scene outside a regional food bank distribution warehouse on the edge of a city: loading bays open, a refrigerated truck backed in, pallets of boxed food staged under sodium lights, volunteers in reflective vests guiding a long line of cars, wet pavement reflecting the warehouse glow, grocery-store delivery trailers visible in the distance, no political signs, no maps, no close-up poverty portrait, documentary realism with the infrastructure of food aid dominating the frame.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Grocery Card Has Become a State Budget Line
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Grocery Card Has Become a State Budget Line. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Premium Sheet Is Becoming a School-Finance Document
articles.summary: California’s new school-healthcare workgroup follows a run of teacher labor fights in which the costly classroom item is not a smart board, a bus or a textbook. It is the promise that the adults in the building can take their families to the doctor without turning a raise into a deduction.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Premium Sheet Is Becoming a School-Finance Document. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Premium Sheet Is Becoming a School-Finance Document
articles.visual_archetype: ground-level human scene with a specific setting and natural light
articles.visual_brief: A low, ground-level view inside a modest elementary school staff room just after sunrise: two school employees sit at a rectangular laminate table beside a window, comparing open health-benefits packets, a coffee mug, a school lanyard, a half-zipped backpack, and a stack of classroom worksheets. Warm natural light falls across their hands and the papers; the background shows a bulletin board with ordinary school notices, slightly out of focus. No posed smiles, no protest signs, no political symbols — just the quiet administrative labor behind a school day.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Premium Sheet Is Becoming a School-Finance Document
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Premium Sheet Is Becoming a School-Finance Document. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Muddy Cup Has a Poison-Center Problem
articles.summary: Kava bars sell a softer night out: no beer taps, no liquor bottles, and a promise of sociability without a hangover. New CDC poison-center data point to a messier 2026 question: what happens when an old Pacific drink becomes a U.S. wellness product, then shares the menu with kratom?
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Muddy Cup Has a Poison-Center Problem. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Muddy Cup Has a Poison-Center Problem
articles.visual_archetype: macro material-detail scene where texture carries the story
articles.visual_brief: Extreme close-up of damp, tan-gray kava root powder gathered in a stained muslin strainer over a shallow ceramic bowl; muddy brown liquid beads through the weave and pools below, with a few pale granules of unlabeled botanical powder on the counter edge. Natural low bar light, high texture, no people, no brand names, no medical props, shallow depth of field.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Muddy Cup Has a Poison-Center Problem
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Muddy Cup Has a Poison-Center Problem. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Build the Room Before the Exit
articles.summary: The assisted-dying bill for England and Wales failed when parliamentary time ran out. The moral question did not. A narrow right to help at the end of life can be defensible, but not if society offers a clean route to death while leaving pain relief, hospice care, disability support and social care unevenly supplied.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Build the Room Before the Exit. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Build the Room Before the Exit
articles.visual_archetype: nonliteral atmospheric scene with color, depth, and one surprising focal object
articles.visual_brief: A dim hospital-family room rendered as an atmospheric, nonliteral scene: deep teal shadows, warm amber light from a half-open interior door, a row of empty visitor chairs receding into soft depth, and one surprising focal object in the foreground — a small brass hotel-style service bell resting on a folded knitted blanket. No people, no medical equipment close-up, no scales, no court buildings. The mood should feel quiet, unsettled and human, with color depth and a sense of choice without literalizing death.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Build the Room Before the Exit
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Build the Room Before the Exit. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Caldera Near Naples Is Being Read One Millimeter at a Time
articles.summary: Campi Flegrei is not sending a simple countdown. Its latest official bulletins show small weekly quakes, continued ground rise, hot fumaroles and a yellow alert — a science story built from instruments, models and the limits of prediction.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Caldera Near Naples Is Being Read One Millimeter at a Time. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Caldera Near Naples Is Being Read One Millimeter at a Time
articles.visual_archetype: institutional room or workplace scene with people, tools, and context
articles.visual_brief: Inside a daylight operations room at a volcanic observatory near Naples: two volcanologists and a civil-protection liaison stand around a long table with printed weekly bulletins, a laptop showing seismogram traces, a GNSS uplift plot and a thermal-camera image of fumaroles. Behind them, wall monitors show waveform lines, instrument-status panels and a live camera feed of a steaming crater area. Include practical details: coffee cups, hard hats on a chair, cable labels, annotated printouts, a whiteboard with dates and millimeters per month, and a printed evacuation-exercise schedule partly visible under a ruler. Human, documentary, not cinematic disaster lighting.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Caldera Near Naples Is Being Read One Millimeter at a Time
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Caldera Near Naples Is Being Read One Millimeter at a Time. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Service Notice Reaches the Closed Crossing
articles.summary: Cambodia’s new military-service bill is being sold as a national-defense repair after last year’s border fighting. Its harder test lies in the same places the conflict has already shaken: migrant wages, household debt, school lists, temple stones, aid ledgers, trucks, observers and the patient regional work of making a ceasefire ordinary.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Service Notice Reaches the Closed Crossing. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Service Notice Reaches the Closed Crossing
articles.visual_archetype: macro material-detail scene where texture carries the story
articles.visual_brief: Extreme close macro photograph of a scuffed Cambodian worker ID card, a folded military-service registration notice, and a frayed plastic border-crossing lanyard resting on dusty concrete beside grains of cassava and a torn bus ticket; shallow depth of field, humid morning light, visible scratches, paper fibers, sweat stains and red dirt, no faces, no flags dominating the frame.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Service Notice Reaches the Closed Crossing
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Service Notice Reaches the Closed Crossing. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: First Fix the Hospital Bill. Then Talk About Collection.
articles.summary: Medical debt may be collectible, but it should not be turned casually into credit data. Congress should replace today’s patchwork with a national rule that makes billing accuracy, insurance processing and financial-assistance screening come before credit punishment.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: First Fix the Hospital Bill. Then Talk About Collection.. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: First Fix the Hospital Bill. Then Talk About Collection.
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: A close, natural-light scene at a community legal-aid or hospital financial-counseling desk: two pairs of adult hands work over an itemized medical bill, an insurance explanation-of-benefits form, a yellow highlighter, a small calculator, a half-filled charity-care application, and a phone on speaker. The focus is on the process of checking codes and totals, not on faces. Warm morning light, paper texture, practical human labor, no logos, no dramatic props.

articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: First Fix the Hospital Bill. Then Talk About Collection.. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The AI Lesson Has to Survive the Training Floor
articles.summary: A federal phone course can make AI literacy less mysterious. The harder test is whether apprenticeships, community colleges and employers turn it into paid practice, worker protection and real mobility.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The AI Lesson Has to Survive the Training Floor. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The AI Lesson Has to Survive the Training Floor

articles.visual_brief: Use the assigned visual archetype (institutional room or workplace scene with people, tools, and context) to show Inside a bright community-college workforce lab during National Apprenticeship Week: an instructor stands beside a wheeled whiteboard with simple prompt-checking steps, while adult apprentices in work shirts compare a phone text lesson, a laptop, a clipboard of apprenticeship standards, safety glasses and a small industrial control panel on a workbench. Include fluorescent ceiling light, coffee cups, a wall clock, tool drawers, union and employer flyers on a bulletin board, and a mix of ages and backgrounds. Documentary realism, no futuristic glow, no a human-scale operations scene with tagged fiber cables, cooling gauges, utility meters, and a technician's gloved hands.

articles.title: A Retired Runway Reopens Greenland’s Arctic Bargain
articles.summary: The newest U.S. push for more Greenland access is not just a base story. It is a three-party argument about missile warning, civilian airfields, ports, old treaty rights, local consent and whether NATO can turn an ally-versus-ally crisis into a colder, calmer plan.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Retired Runway Reopens Greenland’s Arctic Bargain. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Retired Runway Reopens Greenland’s Arctic Bargain

articles.visual_brief: A quiet municipal archive room in Nuuk after hours: a long wooden table under fluorescent lights, opened folders labeled with airfield names, a rolled engineering plan of an Arctic runway, a coffee thermos, wool gloves, a laptop showing a satellite-weather window, and an old black-and-white photograph of a U.S.-built Greenland airstrip pinned beside new Danish and Greenlandic paperwork. No flags as symbols, no map overlay, no soldiers posing.

articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Retired Runway Reopens Greenland’s Arctic Bargain. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Rewrite Fee Lands Inside Hollywood’s Health Plan
articles.summary: The Writers Guild’s new studio contract avoided another shutdown. Its sharper lesson is that a screenplay deal now turns on medical inflation, streaming residuals, AI licenses and the price of staying insured between jobs.

articles.title: A Toothy Keychain Walks Onto the Studio Lot
articles.summary: Labubu’s Sony project is not just a cute-toy headline. It is a live experiment in how a blind-box collectible becomes fashion, platform commerce, investor risk, counterfeit bait and, maybe, a character sturdy enough to survive the jump from handbag to screen.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Toothy Keychain Walks Onto the Studio Lot. AI-generated image by The Press.

No real logos. The mood should feel playful but slightly forensic, as if a toy craze has just become a production meeting.

articles.title: The Inspection Lane Has Become the Walking-Horse Fight
articles.summary: The 2026 Tennessee Walking Horse season is moving under delayed USDA rules, a new animal-welfare lawsuit and an old evidentiary problem: who can tell, at barn speed, whether a famous gait came from breeding, training or pain?
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Inspection Lane Has Become the Walking-Horse Fight. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Inspection Lane Has Become the Walking-Horse Fight
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: Close, documentary-style image inside a horse-show inspection lane at a rural arena: a veterinarian's gloved hands gently lift a Tennessee Walking Horse's front hoof while another hand steadies the pastern above the hoof; a clipboard with inspection forms, a small flashlight, a hoof pick and a measuring gauge sit on a wooden rail; sawdust on the floor, lead rope visible, horse's lower legs in frame, warm overhead barn light, no faces emphasized, no cruelty imagery, focused on the careful physical process of checking soundness.
